Mercedes GLK 220 CDI / 250 CDI Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes GLK 220 CDI / 250 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned GLK 220 CDI engine typically costs £2,500–£4,500 supply only, while a GLK 250 CDI engine costs £3,000–£5,500. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on location and workshop rates. A replacement OM651.912 engine costs on average £3,850 in good condition, with new or reconditioned engines costing up to £4,409. Main dealer new engines start from £8,000–£12,000+ supply only. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes GLK 220 CDI / 250 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM651 is 'well-engineered and generally reliable' and a 'very good engine if well maintained'. With regular oil changes and decent fuel, it can see very high mileages. However, it has documented weaknesses: early 2009 injector problems (resolved by recall), timing chain tensioner wear (cold-start rattle), fuel pressure control valve faults, and oil leaks. The OM651 is considered 'non-rebuildable' due to its spray-coated cylinder walls.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes GLK 220 CDI / 250 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: Fuel injector failure (early 2009 models), timing chain tensioner wear causing cold-start rattle, fuel quantity control valve and injector leaks, oil leaks from various seals, and DPF/emissions system problems from short urban journeys.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes GLK 220 CDI / 250 CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £5,000–£12,000 GLK, a £3,500–£4,500 recon is 30–90% of the car's value. For a clean example with full service history, replacement is often worth it given the GLK's strong reputation for reliability. However, if the car has other issues, evaluate total repair costs before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes GLK 220 CDI / 250 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The GLK 220 CDI uses OM651.912 or OM651.916 (170 hp). The GLK 250 CDI uses OM651 DE22 LA (204 hp). The GLK 220 CDI is available in rear-wheel drive (204.902) and 4MATIC (204.984, 204.997). The GLK 250 CDI is 4MATIC only (204.982, 204.904). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM651 timing chain, or does it need engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative tensioner or chain replacement (£700–£1,500) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain failure (£4,000–£7,000 engine replacement). At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Excessive use of the Mercedes stop-start system is often blamed for timing chain wear. The OM651 is considered non-rebuildable, so if the chain snaps, a full engine replacement is usually the only option.

eraMap.title: Mercedes GLK 220 CDI / 250 CDI — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 2.1L four-cylinder — 170 hp, 400 Nm. Injector issues (early cars), timing chain tensioner wear, and oil leaks are key concerns
2.1L four-cylinder twin-turbo — 204 hp, 500 Nm. Higher output variant of OM651; similar reliability profile to 220 CDI

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes GLK 220 CDI / 250 CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a GLK diesel engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Timing chain and injector work requires specialist attention.

problems.title: Fuel Injector Failure (Early 2009 Models)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, misfires, hard starting, loss of power, increased fuel consumption, black smoke, engine warning light
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear

problems.recommendation: The 2009 220 CDI engine was plagued by frequent injector problems. A large recall campaign carried out in 2010 resolved most of these issues, but replacement injectors are costly. Genuine used injectors are available from £80, with new ones costing more. If you experience rough idle or misfires, have the injectors tested. Catching this early prevents more serious engine damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full injector failure guide

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (OM651)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or ticking noise on cold start for 2–5 seconds, metallic noise that subsides as oil pressure builds, engine performance issues, check engine light
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles — can occur with infrequent oil changes or stop-start system use

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The OM651 timing chain can be a "Dauerbaustelle" (permanent problem area). Cold-start rattling can lead to "kapitalen Motorschäden" (catastrophic engine damage). Excessive use of the Mercedes stop-start system is often blamed. The chain can last well over 300,000 km with proper care, but regular oil changes are essential. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, have the tensioner inspected immediately. A £700–£1,500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than a £4,000–£7,000 engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide → /failures/om651-timing-chain/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Fuel Pressure & Fuel Quantity Control Valve Issues
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, engine won't rev above 1,500 rpm, limp mode, check engine light, rough running

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the engine loses power and won't rev, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: A 2011 GLK 220 CDI owner reported "fuel quantity control valve faults are very common". It could be an injector leak too, where the control cannot follow pressure fluctuation and triggers a fault code. If you experience loss of power, have the fuel system inspected immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full fuel pressure failure guide → /failures/om651-fuel-pressure/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Oil Leaks (Various Seals)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the car, burning oil smell from engine bay, visible oil residue around the engine, low oil level warning
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection if you notice oil spots or burning smell

problems.recommendation: Oil loss through faulty seals is a documented weakness. The OM651 is considered "non-rebuildable" due to its spray-coated cylinder walls, so preventing oil loss through regular seal maintenance is critical. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the seals inspected promptly.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide → /failures/om651-oil-leaks/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: GLK 220 CDI / 250 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Straightforward replacement; recall may have covered early cars
Preventative — catches the fault before catastrophic failure
Full engine replacement is the only reliable fix
Catch early to prevent fuel system damage
Catch early to prevent oil loss and engine damage

buyingChecks.title: Buying This GLK 220 CDI / 250 CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using MB-approved oil
* No cold-start rattling — this is a critical warning sign
* Evidence of regular motorway driving (DPF health)
* No fuel pressure or injector warning codes
avoidIf: * 2009 model with no injector recall history — this is a known weak point
* Cold-start rattle with no documented timing chain work
* Poor service history — the OM651 is a good engine but needs proper maintenance
* Check engine light with fuel pressure or timing-related codes
* DPF warning lights or emissions system warnings

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The GLK 220 CDI and 250 CDI use the OM651 — a "well-engineered and generally reliable" diesel that is "very good if well maintained". The 220 CDI is rear-wheel drive with 170 hp; the 250 CDI is 4MATIC with 204 hp and a two-stage turbocharger. Both share the same engine family. The OM651 has well-documented weaknesses: early injector issues (resolved by recall), timing chain tensioner wear, fuel pressure control valve faults, and oil leaks. Buy a post-2010 model with full service history and no rattling. Without history? Proceed with caution — the OM651 is considered non-rebuildable due to its spray-coated cylinder walls.

cta.label: → Compare GLK 220 CDI / 250 CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes GLK 220 CDI or 250 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or ticking from the engine (timing chain tensioner warning — this is critical)
* Injector History — 2009 models: was the injector recall completed?
* Service History — Including the OM651 engine and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* Fuel Pressure — Any loss of power, limp mode, or inability to rev above 1,500 rpm?
* Oil Leaks — Look for oil residue around the engine (various seals)
* DPF Health — Check for DPF warning lights; short journeys are a red flag
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Look for injector, fuel pressure or timing-related codes
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
